reforms.description: Luxembourg has simplified the 'Fit4' programmes to encourage small enterprises to invest in innovation and technology adoption, while also introducing a new investment tax credit scheme that increases the tax credit rate from 8% to 12% and provides an 18% credit for specific investments. Additionally, the fiscal incentive schemes for private investment in enterprises and start-ups are being remodeled to better align with OECD practices.
reforms.growth_orientation_rationale: These reforms are designed to enhance innovation and productivity among small enterprises, which is expected to positively impact economic growth.
reforms.status_evidence: The reforms have been put into practice as of 2024.
reforms.importance_rationale: The reforms represent significant changes to existing support mechanisms for innovation but do not constitute a complete overhaul of the system.
reforms.source_quote: The reform has increased the tax credit rate from 8% to 12% for generic investment, introducing a 18% tax credit for investments and operating expenses connected with digital and ecological transformation.

reforms.description: Luxembourg is reforming its public innovation support system to streamline R&D support by shifting from institution-based to project-oriented support and enhancing public-private partnerships. This consolidation aims to make innovation support more mission-oriented and increase business R&D expenditure.
reforms.growth_orientation_rationale: By enhancing the effectiveness of public R&D support, the reforms are expected to stimulate private investment in innovation, thereby fostering economic growth.
reforms.status_evidence: The reforms have been officially announced and are set to be implemented in 2025.
reforms.importance_rationale: This reform represents a significant structural change in how public innovation support is organized and delivered.
reforms.source_quote: Consolidating coordination of innovation support, making support more mission-oriented and further fostering public-private partnerships would help raise business R&D expenditure from very low levels.

reforms.description: Luxembourg is strengthening quality standards for training providers to enhance workforce skills, which is crucial for innovation and productivity growth. Additionally, a job-search visa has been introduced for non-EU students who have completed their studies, allowing them to remain in the country for up to 12 months to seek employment or start a business.
reforms.growth_orientation_rationale: Improving workforce skills and attracting international talent are expected to enhance innovation capacity and economic productivity.
reforms.status_evidence: The reforms have been announced and are set to be implemented in 2025.
reforms.importance_rationale: These initiatives represent a significant commitment to enhancing human capital in Luxembourg.
reforms.source_quote: A job-search visa has been introduced for non-EU students who have completed their diploma, allowing them to look for a job or set up a business.

reforms.description: Luxembourg aims to enhance its public support for R&D by increasing the effectiveness of government financial support to encourage business investment in innovation, moving beyond its current reliance on direct funding. The Luxembourg National Research Fund (FNR) has committed to funding public research activities with a ceiling of EUR 445 million from 2022 to 2025, supporting various research projects through grants and direct funding.
reforms.growth_orientation_rationale: By enhancing public R&D funding and effectiveness, the reforms are expected to stimulate private sector investment in innovation.
reforms.status_evidence: The reforms have been announced and are set to be implemented in 2024.
reforms.importance_rationale: This reform represents a significant change in the approach to public R&D funding in Luxembourg.
reforms.source_quote: The Luxembourg National Research Fund (FNR) is the main funder of public research activities, with funding commitments that can reach up to an overall ceiling of EUR 445 million over the years 2022-2025.

reforms.description: Luxembourg is enhancing its research infrastructure through various initiatives, including the Plan national de mobilité 2035, which aims to expand the public transport network and improve infrastructure with an investment of up to 1.7% of GDP between 2023 and 2027. Additionally, subsidy schemes for the purchase and installation of private charging stations for electric vehicles and publicly accessible charging infrastructures have been introduced to support the adoption of electric vehicles.
reforms.growth_orientation_rationale: Investing in transport and charging infrastructure is expected to facilitate innovation and adoption of new technologies, contributing to economic growth.
reforms.status_evidence: The reforms have been implemented as of 2023.
reforms.importance_rationale: These initiatives represent a significant investment in the country's infrastructure, which is crucial for supporting innovation.
reforms.source_quote: The authorities have put in place a structured plan (Klimabonus programme) of financial and non-financial incentives to encourage owners to undertake energy-saving renovations.

reforms.description: The Luxembourg Future Fund 1, a EUR 150 million fund created in collaboration with the European Investment Fund, aims to attract venture capital investment to support the sustainable development of SMEs in strategic sectors.
reforms.growth_orientation_rationale: Attracting venture capital investment is expected to enhance the growth and sustainability of SMEs, contributing to overall economic development.
reforms.status_evidence: The fund has been announced and is set to be implemented in 2024.
reforms.importance_rationale: While the fund represents a significant initiative, it does not constitute a major overhaul of the venture capital landscape.
reforms.source_quote: The Luxembourg Future Fund 1, which is a EUR 150 million fund that aims to attract venture capital investment to foster the sustainable development of SMEs in Luxembourg’s strategic sectors.

reforms.description: The individual training leave scheme in Luxembourg is set to be reformed to broaden its impact by enhancing customization and targeting of financial incentives to support vulnerable groups.
reforms.growth_orientation_rationale: Enhancing the training leave scheme is expected to improve workforce skills and support vulnerable groups, contributing to overall productivity.
reforms.status_evidence: The reform has been announced and is set to be implemented in 2024.
reforms.importance_rationale: The reform represents a significant adjustment to the existing training leave scheme but does not constitute a major overhaul.
reforms.source_quote: The need to reform the individual training leave scheme to broaden its impact has been recognised in the 2023-2028 coalition agreement.

reforms.description: In 2023, Luxembourg reformed its insolvency framework to promote business continuity and entrepreneurship by introducing a debt discharge mechanism for good-faith debtors and enhancing preventive measures against bankruptcy.
reforms.growth_orientation_rationale: The reform aims to support entrepreneurship and business continuity, which are essential for economic growth.
reforms.status_evidence: The reform has been implemented as of 2023.
reforms.importance_rationale: This reform represents a significant change in the insolvency framework, promoting a more supportive environment for businesses.
reforms.source_quote: The 2023 reform is a major positive step as it aims to foster company reorganisation over liquidation, promoting business continuity.
